---
title: "Unit 1 — Linear Models & Model Selection"
subtitle: "One dataset, three fits: scalar least squares → matrix normal equations → cross-validated polynomial"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

Linear models are the foundation of statistical machine learning, and the clearest way to
meet them is on a real trend you can see. In this unit you'll model a genuine time series —
the **men's Olympic 100 m winning times** (1896 onward) — and fit it three increasingly
general ways, watching one idea, *least squares*, carry from a two-number scalar formula all
the way to a cross-validated polynomial. By the end you'll have built the regression machinery
the rest of the course depends on, and seen first-hand why choosing a model is a question you
answer with data, not taste.

## Learning objectives

By the end of Unit 1 you will be able to:

- **Fit a scalar least-squares line** $t \approx w_0 + w_1 x$ by the closed-form normal
  equations. *(Apply)*
- **Fit the same model in matrix form**, $\mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{t}$,
  *applying* the matrix-calculus gradient identities. *(Apply)*
- **Build polynomial features** with correct scaling to $[0, 1]$. *(Apply)*
- **Select the model order by cross-validation.** *(Analyze)*
- **Interpret the result** — the CV curve in bias–variance terms, the limits of
  extrapolation, and when cross-validation can mislead. *(Evaluate)*

## What you'll do

You'll fit the Olympic 100 m data three ways, each deepening the last:

- **(A) Scalar least squares** — the two-parameter line $t \approx w_0 + w_1 x$, in closed
  form.
- **(B) The matrix normal equation** — the same fit written with a design matrix
  $\mathbf{X} = [\,\mathbf{1}\ \ \mathbf{x}\,]$. At order 1 it **must agree with Part A
  exactly** (both predict ≈ **9.59 s** for 2012) — that agreement is the through-line that
  ties the unit together.
- **(C) Polynomial features + cross-validation** — add higher-order columns, scale to
  $[0, 1]$, and let **5-fold cross-validation** choose the order (scaling each fold *after*
  the split, so no information leaks).

Then write three short interpretation answers: which order CV chose and why, what the curves
say about overfitting, and one situation where CV here could mislead. Any sound method earns
full credit — the closed-form normal equations, `np.linalg.lstsq`, or `np.polyfit` — but
**seed the CV shuffle** (`np.random.default_rng(0)`) and don't hard-code the target numbers.

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** NumPy array basics and linear algebra — matrix multiply, transpose, and
solving a linear system (`np.linalg.solve`); and mean-squared error as a loss. Unit 0
(onboarding) should be passed before you start.

**Connections.**

> Part B **applies** the matrix-calculus gradient identities
> $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{a}) = \mathbf{a}$ and
> $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}$ to reach
> the normal equation. You **use them here; you prove them in Unit 2b**, which derives those
> identities in general — a deliberate *use-then-prove* arc.

The design-matrix thinking and the cross-validation workflow you build here carry forward into
the model-selection and Bayesian units later in the course.

## How it's graded

This unit is graded by **specifications, not points**. Your work is checked against three
bundles — **Correctness** (autograded method-validity and outcome checks), **Interpretation**
(your written CERL answers: Claim, Evidence, Reasoning, Limits), and **Process**
(reproducibility and the submission contract). Each bundle either passes or comes back once
for **revise-and-resubmit**; the unit passes when all three do, and Correctness resubmissions
are unlimited until the deadline. The full policy and the letter-grade ladder are in
[`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md) — read it once.

## What to submit

- Your completed **`hw.py`** — the implementations the autograder imports
  (`SimpleLinearModel`, `PolynomialRegressionModel`, `scale`, `run_K_fold_cv`), with their
  signatures unchanged.
- Your **rendered notebook** — the unit's `.qmd` run top-to-bottom, including the
  cross-validation figure and your I1–I3 interpretation answers.

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **6–9 hours [PROPOSED]** — this unit consolidates three former homeworks (scalar
regression, matrix/polynomial regression, and scaling + cross-validation) into one connected
arc, so most of the time goes into the Part C cross-validation and the written interpretations
rather than into three separate setups.
